title: The Strange Case of 'Influence Peddling' in Candidate Voineag's Resume: Intoxication or Kompromat at DNA?
summary: The article critically examines a DNA (National Anticorruption Directorate) case against lawyer Adriana Georgescu, accused of influence peddling involving President Nicușor Dan and PM Ilie Bolojan, arguing the case is extremely thin on evidence. The author raises serious questions about whether the case was manufactured as a pretext to surveil Romania's top leadership, potentially serving as kompromat useful to Russia's interests in discrediting anti-Russian EU governments.
talking_points: The DNA case against lawyer Adriana Georgescu appears to lack substantive evidence - she promised influence she couldn't deliver, making it closer to fraud than influence peddling
The case may have been deliberately constructed to enable legal surveillance of President Nicușor Dan and PM Ilie Bolojan's institutional circles over 5 months
The Bucharest Court of Appeal's jurisdiction was questionably invoked through Georgescu's dormant lawyer status, possibly to access friendly judges
The author questions whether DNA chief candidate Voineag orchestrated this during the electoral campaign for the anti-corruption chief prosecutor position
If surveillance of Romania's president and prime minister occurred based on unverified information, it would be an unprecedented democratic violation, potentially serving Russian kompromat interests
concepts: electoral campaign manipulation through criminal cases
related_concepts: rule of law and judicial independence
Russian intelligence operations in EU countries
author: Radu Ştefăniță
